sections.title: Global Equities
sections.content: Global markets MSCI World -1.53%, Developed Markets -1.49%, Emerging Markets -1.78%; S&P 500 -2.12% and MAG7 -4.42%; European Indices Euro Stoxx 50 +0.08%; In Aisa, China CSI -1.41%  and Hang Seng -1.29%, Nikkei 225 flat.

sections.title: US Equities
sections.content: US equity indexes concluded a volatile, headline-driven week with a marked divergence in size and style performance. S&P 500, Dow, and Nasdaq each recorded their fifth consecutive weekly decline. Energy emerged as the standout sector by a wide margin, buoyed by Middle East supply risks, while value stocks continued to outperform their growth counterparts. On the macro front, the Flash Composite PMI moderated to an 11-month low of 51.4, reflecting a deceleration in services activity despite manufacturing output strengthening modestly. Consumer sentiment also faltered, with the University of Michigan index dropping to 53.3 as inflation expectations for the year ahead ticked up to 3.8%.

sections.title: European Equities
sections.content: European equities faced intense selling pressure. Investor sentiment was severely hampered by the escalation of conflict in the Middle East, specifically involving strategic attacks on energy infrastructure in the Strait of Hormuz and natural gas terminals in Qatar. In response to the persistent energy shock, the ECB and BoE both held interest rates steady at their respective policy meetings. The ECB notably upwardly revised its 2026 inflation forecast to 2.6%, acknowledging that soaring fuel costs are complicating the disinflationary trajectory. Furthermore, the euro area’s trade deficit widened to EUR 1.9 billion in January, driven by a contraction in machinery and chemical exports as geopolitical uncertainty dampened industrial output.

sections.title: APAC Equities
sections.content: APAC equity markets suffered broad declines. Chinese stocks were weighed down by the dual burden of soaring energy costs and fragile domestic demand, as limited policy support failed to provide a meaningful floor. In Japan, the Nikkei 225 experienced mid-week relief on de-escalation hopes but finished the period volatile as the BoJ maintained its policy rate at 0.75%. The central bank emphasised the need to monitor Middle East volatility and imported inflation from high oil prices, despite inflation temporarily moderating below 2%. While the yen strengthened modestly to JPY 159.2, it remains weak by historical standards, offering a double-edged sword for exporters facing rising input costs. Broadly, regional sentiment remains hostage to the evolving energy crisis and its impact on trade-sensitive economies.

sections.title: Foreign Exchange
sections.content: The global FX landscape is increasingly moving to a Middle East rhythm, as the USD maintains its safe-haven appeal and benefits from the US position as a net energy exporter. The dollar index climbed approximately 0.4% this week, bolstered by Trump’s extension of strike deadlines on Iranian infrastructure and the breakdown of ceasefire proposals. In contrast, the EUR remained range-bound between 1.14 and 1.15, lacking the fundamental momentum to break its 21-day moving average amid weakening Eurozone PMIs and rising price pressures. Sterling’s performance saw UK gilt yields climb as markets priced in inflationary spillovers; however, while higher yields supported the pound, growth concerns related to a supply-led inflation impulse tempered the upside. Meanwhile, the AUD led the G10 pack despite broader volatility, as FX shifts back to terms-of-trade dynamics. Overall, currency markets favour defensive positioning as diplomatic efforts remain fractured.

sections.title: Commodities
sections.content: The commodities complex is undergoing a regime shift as a tangible energy shock evolves into a broader commodity inflation cycle. The energy subindex has surged nearly 41% this month, with Brent crude hovering around $108 per barrel due to the effective disruption of flows through the Strait of Hormuz. A critical development is the depletion of “oil on water,” as cargo buffers erode and rerouting around the Cape of Good Hope increases costs and shipping times. Second-round effects are now lifting industrial metals and agriculture, with copper and grains broadly higher as focus shifts to rising input costs and production risks. Conversely, precious metals faced a liquidity-driven correction, with gold shedding 2.5% as a firmer USD and rising yields overshadowed safe-haven demand. This divergence highlights a transition from a futures-driven risk repricing to a genuine physical shortage across essential raw materials.

sections.title: Fixed Income
sections.content: Fixed income yields adjusted higher as investors digested a policy pause from the Fed and hawkish revisions to central bank forecasts. The yield on the benchmark 10-year UST climbed to approximately 4.39%, its highest level in eight months, as the market priced in the risk that the Fed may be forced to hike rates to counter energy-driven inflation. The Fed maintained the benchmark rate at 3.50% to 3.75%, but updated projections showed median estimates for just one rate cut in 2026, while GDP and inflation forecasts were revised upward. Credit markets remained relatively resilient, with IG spreads holding firm despite broader volatility, though the US labour market appears to be slowing to a crawl. With hiring moderating and unemployment stabilising near 4.3%, policymakers face a delicate balancing act between managing an energy-led inflation outburst and protecting growth against tightening financial conditions.
